old_claim.note: Commercial Auction Houses provides a capability that enables this technology without being the only possible path.

new_claim_absent: No direct dependency remains because auction houses are one possible advertiser category, not the newspaper publication mechanism for paid public notices.

source_shape.source_locator: MHS says the first paid advertisements in issue number three concerned stolen goods, lost anvils, and Long Island real estate, while the first issue had already solicited paid notices at a reasonable rate.
source_shape.source_claim_summary: The source grounds the first paid newspaper advertisements in newspaper solicitation and miscellaneous notices, not in auction-house infrastructure.
source_shape.source_support_rationale: Auction services may later advertise in newspapers, but the source-backed mechanism for the scoped node is insertion of paid notices into a recurring newspaper.

ontology_before: Public Newspaper Advertising depended directly on Commercial Auction Houses, implying auction-house institutions enabled the newspaper advertising format.
ontology_after: Public Newspaper Advertising depends directly on newspaper periodicals; auction houses are treated as possible later advertisers or content categories rather than prerequisites.
invariant_preserved_or_changed: Changed: public_newspaper_advertising no longer directly depends on commercial_auction_houses.
would_reject_if: The node is later narrowed specifically to auction advertisements in newspapers.
A stronger source shows early paid newspaper advertising was created to serve organized auction-house notices.
The graph adds a separate auction-newspaper-notice node where commercial_auction_houses is the scoped advertiser infrastructure.
short_reason: Auction houses were a possible ad customer category, not the enabling publication mechanism.
